### Post by deathshadow on 2007-03-03
Assuming you are running single output, you really shouldn't need to be futzing with your xorg.conf to get that card up and running.

go into synaptic, Enable the multiverse (might as well hit the universe too, Settings > Repositories), install the nvidia-glx package (which should auto pickup the other needed modules)... I suggest a reboot at this point (that way any kernel changes actually take), when the login manager comes up hit CTRL-ALT-F1 to switch to a terminal session, login, do a 

sudo nvidia-xconfig

log out, hit CTRL-ALT-F7 to return to the login manager, hit ctrl-backspace to kill X, the login manager at this point should reload X with the glx driver installed. End of story.

It seems a lot of people miss the 'nvidia-xconfig' part... Of course it doesn't help the multiple conflicting instructions on doing it from the command line from version to version and distro to distro... Especially when 90%+ of the setup can be done from Synaptic.

---

### Post by bballs91 on 2007-03-04
Nah didn't work, after installing the nvidia-glx i rebooted, then I ran the sudo nvidia-xconfig.  Then i rebooted again and it gave me an error saying no screens found.  So I had to change the driver back to vesa manually in xorg.conf in order to be able to boot back into ubuntu.

EDIT: I think i have it correctly installed.  Automatix2 says it is, and it placed an Nvidia settings link in system tools.  The only thing is it won't boot correctly when I set nvidia as the driver...

---

### Post by bballs91 on 2007-03-04
Sorry for the triple post :).  I finally got it working though!  I just cleaned everything, reinstalled the kernel then ran envy again.

### Post by deathshadow on 2007-03-05
> **bballs91 said:**
> Nah didn't work, after installing the nvidia-glx i rebooted, then I ran the sudo nvidia-xconfig.  Then i rebooted again and it gave me an error saying no screens found.  So I had to change the driver back to vesa manually in xorg.conf in order to be able to boot back into ubuntu.

EDIT: I think i have it correctly installed.  Automatix2 says it is, and it placed an Nvidia settings link in system tools.  The only thing is it won't boot correctly when I set nvidia as the driver...

Well, right off - use Synaptic and not automatix2. Automatix2 is cute for applications, but I wouldn't trust it to install a driver as far as I could throw a sumo wrestler.

Second, pull your /var/log/xorg.0.log file after it comes up with no screens found, so we can see the ACTUAL error it's producing. I suspect either incorrect monitor timings, or the driver is fubar/broken/missing essential componants. (again why I prefer to use synaptic since it seems better at resolving the dependancies)

It could be as simple as the nvidia-xconfig not setting up your monitor correctly (a UBER common X problem that there's REALLY no excuse for any more), or as complex as some sub-module of the kernel or drivers not being present/in correctly/ wrong version. (in other words, welcome to dependancy hell)

Worst comes to worst - CLEAN install of Edgy, follow the instructions I posted EXACTLY, should work fine (unless it's the monitor timing issue)

---
